questions.question: Why does picking the wrong metric matter so much in audio?
options: Each audio task has multiple metrics measuring different axes — the wrong one ships a model that looks great on your dashboard and terrible in production (e.g., great WER but unusable first-token latency for streaming)
questions.explanation: The canonical 2026 pairing: ASR → WER (+CER, RTFx, latency), TTS → MOS/UTMOS (+SECS, round-trip WER, TTFA), speaker → EER (+minDCF), diarization → DER, music → FAD, LALMs → MMAU-Pro.

questions.question: Before computing WER, what normalization is required, and when does CER replace it?
options: Lowercase, strip punctuation, normalize numbers (jiwer or whisper_normalizer) — and use character-level CER for tone languages like Mandarin where word segmentation is ambiguous
questions.explanation: Un-normalized scoring inflates WER with formatting differences that no listener would call errors. Under 5% WER is human parity for read speech.

questions.question: What do RTFx and first-token latency measure, and why track both?
options: They are the same number inverted

RTFx is throughput (audio seconds per wall-clock second — Parakeet-TDT: 3380x, Whisper-large-v3: ~30x); first-token latency is responsiveness (audio-in to first transcript token, ~150ms for Deepgram Nova-3) — batch jobs care about the first, streaming about the second
questions.explanation: A model can have superb throughput and terrible streaming latency (or vice versa). The deployment shape decides which one is your primary metric.

questions.question: Why evaluate voice cloning with SECS + MOS + CER as a triple?
options: Three metrics look better in reports

Each catches a different failure: high SECS but low MOS = right timbre but unnatural speech; high MOS but low SECS = natural voice but wrong speaker; CER (via ASR round-trip) catches intelligibility regressions
questions.explanation: SECS > 0.75 means a recognizable clone (ECAPA cosine). UTMOS substitutes for human MOS at ~0.9 correlation; running Whisper over the output and scoring against the input text closes the loop.

questions.question: How is Diarization Error Rate (DER) composed?
options: Word errors divided by speaker count
(False alarm + missed speech + speaker confusion) / total speaker time — AMI-meeting DER of 10-20% is realistic, with pyannote 3.1 and commercial stacks under 10% on well-recorded audio
questions.explanation: DER decomposes "who spoke when" errors into three named failure modes — which one dominates tells you whether to fix VAD (miss/FA) or clustering (confusion).
